  • Hide All Code
  • Download Rmd

R Stats Notebook

Manipulating Data

subset

subsetting keeps it as a data frame

library(ISwR)
data("energy") 
energy2 = subset(energy, expend < 7.5)

qualitative subsetting

energy3 = subset(energy, stature == "obese", 
                 select = expend:structure)

can also separate into two separate vectors:

exp.lean = energy$expend[energy$stature == "lean"]
exp.lean
exp.obese = energy$expend[energy$stature == "obese"] 
exp.obese

Transforming data

energy4 <- transform(energy, log.expend = log(expend))
                    #dataset ##column name ##transformation

Implicit Loops

multiple ways to do this: lapply() –> always returns a list sapply() –> simplifies result into a matrix apply() –> works on matrices not data frames tapply() –> creates a table as output

The Aggregate Function

Summarizing data from one variable based on a second variable (usally a category) aggregate() –> splits the data into subsets, computes summary statistics for each, and returns the result aggregate(x, by, FUN, .., simlify = TRUE)

aggregate(energy$expend, by = list(stature = energy$stature), mean, na.rm = TRUE)

The Tidyverse Method

energy %>% 
  group_by(stature) %>% 
  summarise(mean_value = mean(expend))

returns a tibble format

Importing Data

read.table(file, header = FALSE, sep = ““)

read.table("~/Desktop/Academic/M.S. Biology/Advanced Stats/Adv Stats in R /Owls.txt", header = TRUE)

can also use “read.csv()” for .csv files

Exercise


1. calculate mean arrival time for males and females

library(tidyverse)
Owls %>% 
  group_by(SexParent) %>%
  summarise(mean.value = mean(ArrivalTime))
  1. mean arrival time for each food treatment
library(tidyverse)
Owls %>% 
  group_by(FoodTreatment) %>%
  summarise(mean.value = mean(ArrivalTime))
  1. sep. vectors of farrival times for each sex
male.arrivals <- Owls[Owls$SexParent == "Male", ArrivalTime]

Error: object 'ArrivalTime' not found
  1. sep dataframes of complete data set for each sex
  1. mean arrival time for each sex for each food treatment

Plotting

Veg <- read_csv("Vegetation2.csv")

scatterplot

Can be done two different ways:


or (recommended) using a formula:

Plotting with labels

Able to change the plotting character (pch) to match specific transects in the data

Use of a vector for pch arguments:

Change the character size with ‘cex’

Boxplot

Histogram

Great for visualizing distributions
